First, the animal diversity problem. The world contains approximately 8.7 million species. The flood narrative requires that every land-dwelling species — mammals, birds, reptiles, amphibians, insects — be represented on the ark. The ark's dimensions as described in the biblical text — 300 cubits long, 50 cubits wide, 30 cubits high — would provide approximately 1.5 million square feet of deck space. That's roughly equivalent to 21 football fields. Even with modern engineering, packing 70,000 species onto that footprint while maintaining viable population sizes for post-flood repopulation exceeds biological and spatial constraints.

A flood capable of covering all land surfaces — from mountain peaks to ocean floors — would require water volume exceeding current ocean capacity by orders of magnitude. The notion that all species would recognize a divine command and voluntarily gather at a single location — while simultaneously their predators and prey, their parasites and hosts, their competitors and collaborators — is biologically incoherent. The flood narrative treats animals as passive cargo rather than active ecological participants.
